text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY PANCREATIC CANCER TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE
Medical Oncology — Initial Consultation

ATTENDING MEDICAL ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
SERVICE: Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
CONSULTING SERVICES PRESENT AT TUMOR BOARD: Surgical Oncology (Hepatobiliary), Radiation Oncology, Diagnostic Radiology (Abdominal Imaging), Pathology, Gastroenterology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African female, retired schoolteacher, who presents for initial medical oncology consultation following recent diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). She was referred for multidisciplinary tumor board review and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old nonsmoker with a background of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who came to medical attention over the past several months with a constellation of symptoms that prompted abdominal imaging. She reports a 3-month history of progressive worsening of her previously well-controlled blood sugars, which her primary care physician initially attributed to dietary changes and medication non-adherence. In retrospect, this represents new-onset pancreatogenic diabetes — a recognized early harbinger of pancreatic malignancy — and was the pivotal symptom driving workup. She also endorses vague, dull epigastric and right upper quadrant discomfort that she described as "fullness" and attributed to her diabetes. She notes a 4–5 kg unintentional weight loss over this same period. She denied frank jaundice initially, though family members noted mild scleral icterus on one occasion. No pruritus. No acholic stools or dark urine at the time of initial presentation.

In response to worsening glycemic control, her PCP ordered abdominal ultrasonography, which demonstrated a hypoechoic mass in the head of the pancreas with associated pancreatic duct dilation. She was then urgently referred to gastroenterology, and a pancreatic-protocol multiphasic CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained. This revealed a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas with involvement of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over greater than 180 degrees of circumference and direct abutment of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with mild luminal narrowing. One peripancreatic lymph node measuring 1.2 cm was suspicious for metastatic involvement. There was moderate main pancreatic duct dilation to 6 mm and mild intrahepatic biliary dilation with a common bile duct diameter of 12 mm. No hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or distant organ metastases were identified on CT. A subsequent FDG-PET/CT confirmed an intensely FDG-avid pancreatic head mass (SUVmax 8.9) with one FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2) and no distant metastatic disease.

Tissue confirmation was obtained by endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy, which demonstrated moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (infiltrating duct carcinoma, grade 2) with prominent desmoplastic stroma. IHC showed CK7+, CK20−, CDX2−, MUC1+, and notably, loss of SMAD4 (DPC4) expression. Comprehensive genomic profiling via 324-gene next-generation sequencing panel revealed: KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, ARID1A p.R1276* (VAF 12%), RNF43 p.G659fs (VAF 8%), ATM p.V2716fs frameshift variant (VAF 6% — germline vs. somatic uncertain), and a low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant (c.8331+1G>A, VAF 5% — likely subclonal or technical artifact). TMB was low at 3 mutations/Mb, MSI status was MSS, and PD-L1 CPS was 2. No targetable fusions or HER2 amplification identified.

CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL (markedly elevated). CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. These values will serve as the baseline for tumor marker surveillance.

The case has been formally presented to the Multidisciplinary Pancreatic Cancer Tumor Board today with review of all imaging, pathology, and genomic data. The patient has been seen in clinic for this consultation and participates in treatment planning.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (symptomatic but fully ambulatory).

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago; previously well-controlled on metformin; notable worsening over the past 3 months likely related to underlying malignancy (pancreatogenic component)
2. Hypertension — well-controlled
3. No prior malignancy
4. No prior abdominal surgeries
5. No known history of chronic pancreatitis or pancreatic cysts

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- None of significance; no prior abdominal operations

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: hypertension, died of cardiovascular disease in her 70s
- Father: type 2 diabetes mellitus
- No known family history of pancreatic cancer
- No known family history of breast, ovarian, colorectal, or other Lynch-associated cancers
- Note: Given newly identified ATM frameshift variant (germline vs. somatic uncertain) and low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant, formal genetic counseling referral has been placed. ATM heterozygous germline mutations are associated with modestly increased risk of pancreatic cancer and other malignancies (breast, prostate). Family history does not appear to segregate a hereditary syndrome but is limited.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired schoolteacher; 66-year-old West African female
- Nonsmoker (lifetime)
- Rare social alcohol use; no illicit drug use
- Lives alone; widowed; strong extended family support network (adult children nearby)
- BMI: 24.1 kg/m²
- Independent with activities of daily living
- No known occupational exposures

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes mellitus)
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
3. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
4. Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily (dyslipidemia)
5. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
6. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally once daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Unintentional weight loss of approximately 4–5 kg over 3 months. Fatigue, moderate. Decreased appetite.
- GI: Epigastric fullness and dull right upper quadrant discomfort. No frank jaundice at present visit. No significant nausea or vomiting. Mild steatorrhea noted (greasy, floating stools), possibly indicative of early exocrine insufficiency from pancreatic duct obstruction. No hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia.
- Endocrine: Worsening glycemic control with home glucose readings frequently in 200–280 mg/dL range despite baseline metformin therapy.
- Neurologic: No peripheral neuropathy at baseline. No focal neurologic deficits.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
- Skin: No rash. No jaundice at present examination.
- Psychiatric: Anxious about diagnosis. Appropriate affect. Supportive family.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16 breaths/min, Temperature 37.1°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 61.2 kg, Height 163 cm, BMI 23.0 kg/m² (noting weight loss from prior BMI 24.1).

General: Pleasant, well-groomed West African female appearing her stated age. Alert and oriented x3. No acute distress. Mild anxious affect; engaged in discussion.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No scleral icterus at this visit. No temporal wasting. Oral mucosa moist. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Neck: No supraclavicular, cervical, or submandibular adenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or rales.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the epigastric and right upper quadrant regions. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass on exam (consistent with deep pancreatic location of tumor). No ascites by percussion. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No peripheral lymphadenopathy in the inguinal regions.

Skin: No jaundice. No palmar erythema. No spider angiomata. No rash.

Neurologic: Alert and cognitively intact. Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No focal sensory or motor deficits. No baseline peripheral neuropathy on exam — this is important to document prior to initiating Eloxatin-based chemotherapy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (reviewed today and at time of initial workup):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.4 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, consistent with chronic disease/cancer)
- Platelets: 312 x10^9/L (normal)
- Neutrophils: 4.8 x10^9/L (ANC adequate for chemotherapy)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Total bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL (upper limit of normal; biliary dilation present on imaging but not yet causing frank hyperbilirubinemia)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- ALT: 52 U/L (mildly elevated, likely biliary involvement)
- AST: 44 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 184 U/L (elevated, consistent with biliary compression)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (low normal, consistent with nutritional depletion)
- Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (GFR estimated >60 mL/min/1.73m²)
- Glucose: 214 mg/dL (poorly controlled at this visit)
- HbA1c: 9.6% (reflecting worsening glycemic control over prior 3 months)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 1,247 U/mL (markedly elevated; used as baseline)
- CEA: 8.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated)

Coagulation:
- INR: 1.1 (normal)
- PTT: 29 seconds (normal)

Other: CA 19-9 Lewis antigen status: Patient confirmed Lewis antigen-positive (no false-negative risk). Hepatitis B surface antigen: negative. Hepatitis B surface antibody: positive (immune). Hepatitis C antibody: negative.

---

IMAGING AND PATHOLOGY REVIEWED AT TUMOR BOARD (summary):

Multiphasic pancreatic-protocol CT (Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis): 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass, pancreatic head/uncinate. SMA contact >180 degrees circumference. SMV abutment with mild luminal narrowing, no complete occlusion. Main pancreatic duct dilation 6 mm, CBD dilation 12 mm. Peripancreatic node 1.2 cm, suspicious. No hepatic, peritoneal, or distant metastases.

FDG-PET/CT: Intensely FDG-avid primary pancreatic mass (SUVmax 8.9), one FDG-avid peripancreatic node (SUVmax 4.2). No distant metastatic foci.

EUS-FNB Pathology (reviewed with pathology): Moderately differentiated PDAC, grade 2. Desmoplastic stroma. CK7+, CK20−, CDX2−, MUC1+. SMAD4/DPC4 loss by IHC. Ki-67 ~35%.

NGS (324-gene panel, tissue): KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, ARID1A R1276*, RNF43 G659fs, ATM V2716fs (VAF 6%, germline uncertain), BRCA2 splice variant (VAF 5%, low — likely subclonal or artifact). TMB-low, MSS, PD-L1 CPS 2. No actionable fusions or HER2 amplification.

Tumor Board consensus staging: Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, T4 N1 M0, Stage III (AJCC 8th edition).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — Stage III (T4 N1 M0)

Ms. Osei-Bonsu presents with newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed, borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. The classification as borderline resectable is driven primarily by SMA involvement exceeding 180 degrees of circumference — a feature that by consensus criteria (AHPBA/SSAT/SSO, NCCN) places this tumor beyond technically resectable into borderline territory. Direct SMV contact with partial luminal narrowing is also noted. While immediate upfront surgery is not appropriate, the absence of distant metastatic disease and her good performance status make her a candidate for multimodality treatment with curative intent.

The molecular profile is typical for sporadic PDAC: KRAS G12V driver mutation (the most common KRAS codon 12 variant), TP53 R248W loss-of-function, and co-deletion of both SMAD4 and CDKN2A — a profile associated with aggressive biology but no immediately actionable therapeutic targets. Importantly, she is MSS/TMB-low, ruling out a checkpoint inhibitor strategy (Keytruda, nivo). SMAD4 loss portends a slightly higher risk of systemic dissemination and may temper expectations for local control alone, though we will proceed with curative intent. The ATM frameshift variant with low VAF is ambiguous; germline confirmation is pending and will be important for family counseling and, if confirmed, potential eligibility for PARP inhibitor maintenance strategies post-resection in future settings. The low-VAF BRCA2 splice site variant is likely subclonal or artifactual, but germline testing is warranted to rule out actionable hereditary contribution.

PLAN FOR NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY:
After multidisciplinary discussion with surgical oncology, radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology, the consensus recommendation is for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy prior to any consideration of surgery or radiation. The rationale is multi-fold: (a) to achieve tumor reduction and ideally improve vascular anatomy, rendering the patient more favorably positioned for R0 resection; (b) to treat potential micrometastatic disease early; and (c) to serve as an in vivo test of disease biology — patients who progress on neoadjuvant therapy are unlikely to benefit from surgery.

Regimen: Modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX)
- Eloxatin 85 mg/m²
- Camptosar 150 mg/m² (dose-reduced from the standard 180 mg/m² given the patient's age of 66 and underlying type 2 diabetes, which may increase susceptibility to GI toxicity and neuropathy)
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m²
- 5FU 2400 mg/m² as a 46-hour continuous infusion
- Bolus 5-FU OMITTED (consistent with standard mFOLFIRINOX modification to reduce toxicity)
- Cycle every 14 days; planned 8 cycles (approximately 4 months)

Rationale for mFOLFIRINOX over gem/nab-pac: Given her ECOG 1 performance status, adequate organ function, and the potential benefit of the more active triplet backbone in the neoadjuvant setting, mFOLFIRINOX is preferred per current NCCN guidelines and institutional protocol. Published data (FOLFIRINOX vs. gem in metastatic PDAC; Conroy et al.) and neoadjuvant series support the superior activity of FOLFIRINOX-based regimens in patients who can tolerate them.

Supportive care and toxicity prophylaxis:
- Prophylactic pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) on day 2 of each cycle given the myelosuppressive potential of iri and Eloxatin
- Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, olanzapine 5 mg (NK1/5-HT3/steroid combination for highly emetogenic chemotherapy)
- Loperamide (Imodium) PRN for diarrhea with written management guidelines provided to patient
- Patient educated on cold sensitivity from oxali (avoidance of cold exposure to hands and mouth during and after infusions for first several days)
- Baseline peripheral neuropathy neurologic exam documented (no neuropathy at baseline)

2. BILIARY MONITORING AND INTERVENTION PLANNING
Total bilirubin currently 1.1 mg/dL, mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase. Although not requiring immediate biliary decompression, we will monitor LFTs closely. If bilirubin rises to 2 mg/dL or above, or if the patient develops symptomatic jaundice or cholangitis, biliary stenting (ERCP-guided metal stent preferred for durable drainage in a patient anticipating extended neoadjuvant treatment) will be arranged urgently through gastroenterology. She should be instructed to report any onset of jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stools, or fever.

3. GENETIC COUNSELING AND GERMLINE TESTING
The NGS panel identified an ATM p.V2716fs frameshift variant (VAF 6%) and a BRCA2 splice site variant c.8331+1G>A (VAF 5%). Both have low VAFs and are ambiguous regarding germline vs. somatic origin. Formal genetic counseling referral has been placed today. Germline testing will be sent (preferably blood or saliva-based germline panel). If ATM germline pathogenic variant is confirmed, this has implications for: (a) family member testing (autosomal dominant inheritance), (b) potential eligibility for PARP inhibitor strategies in maintained therapy contexts (ola in BRCA/ATM-mutated pancreatic cancer — POLO trial data support BRCA2, and emerging data for ATM in maintenance), and (c) ongoing surveillance considerations for breast and other cancers. Patient has been briefly counseled on this today; full discussion at genetic counseling appointment.

4. DIABETES MANAGEMENT OPTIMIZATION
HbA1c is 9.6%, indicating significantly worsening glycemic control — partially attributable to underlying pancreatogenic diabetes from pancreatic duct obstruction and tumor-related mechanisms. Metformin should be held during chemotherapy infusions (IV contrast exposure risk; also lactic acidosis risk in the setting of potential GI toxicity and dehydration). Endocrinology co-management referral placed for optimization of glycemic control during chemotherapy. Adequate diabetes management is important for chemotherapy tolerance, wound healing considerations for future surgery, and overall performance status.

5. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT
Patient has lost 4–5 kg over 3 months. Albumin is 3.6 g/dL. Evidence of early pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhea). Dietitian referral placed. Supplemental oral nutritional support (Ensure Plus or similar) encouraged. Consider empiric pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (e.g., Creon/pancrelipase) if steatorrhea is bothersome. Adequate nutritional status is critical for tolerance of mFOLFIRINOX and future surgical candidacy.

6. RESTAGING AND RESPONSE ASSESSMENT PLAN
CA 19-9 is established as baseline at 1,247 U/mL today. Tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA) will be checked with each cycle. Formal restaging with multiphasic pancreatic-protocol CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 2 months from start of treatment). Response assessment will guide continuation of therapy, modification of plan, or consideration of early progression.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PATIENT EDUCATION
Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her family (adult children accompanied her today) were counseled at length about the diagnosis, the intent of neoadjuvant therapy (to improve the chances of achieving a successful surgery with negative margins), the expected side effects of mFOLFIRINOX (nausea, fatigue, neuropathy, diarrhea, alopecia, myelosuppression), and the overall treatment trajectory. She understands that the ultimate goal is to achieve sufficient response to allow for a potentially curative surgery (Whipple procedure), while acknowledging that outcomes in pancreatic cancer are uncertain. She expressed a strong desire to pursue aggressive treatment and accepted the recommendation. She will have a dedicated nurse navigator and will have direct phone access to the oncology clinic for toxicity management.

Social work referral placed for psychosocial support. Patient resides alone but has strong family support and transportation arranged.

---

SUMMARY OF TUMOR BOARD RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Diagnosis confirmed: Borderline resectable PDAC, Stage III (T4 N1 M0)
- Neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) x 8 cycles recommended
- No upfront surgery at this time (SMA >180 degrees renders immediate resection unfeasible)
- Biliary decompression on standby if bilirubin rises
- Genetic counseling referral placed (ATM frameshift, BRCA2 low-VAF variant)
- Endocrinology referral for diabetes optimization
- Dietitian referral for nutritional support
- Restaging CT planned after 4 cycles
- ECOG 1; performance status adequate for mFOLFIRINOX
- No prior systemic therapy; treatment-naive at initiation
- CA 19-9 baseline: 1,247 U/mL
- Cancer present; intent curative

--- 
Electronic signature: [Attending Medical Oncologist, GI Oncology]
Note dictated and signed by attending physician; reviewed with patient and family.
